title: K-Means on Iris Dataset
description: Use K-Means to cluster the iris dataset into 3 clusters and print the inertia.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data

# TODO: Scale the data and fit K-Means with 3 clusters
# TODO: Print the inertia
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data

# Scale the data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Fit K-Means
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42)
kmeans.fit(X_scaled)

print(f"Inertia: {kmeans.inertia_:.2f}")
print(f"Cluster centers shape: {kmeans.cluster_centers_.shape}")
print(f"Cluster labels: {np.unique(kmeans.labels_)}")

title: Finding Optimal K with Elbow Method
description: Use the elbow method to find the optimal number of clusters for the iris dataset.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Test K values from 1 to 10 and store inertias
# TODO: Print the inertia for each K
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

inertias = []
K_range = range(1, 11)

for k in K_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    kmeans.fit(X_scaled)
    inertias.append(kmeans.inertia_)
    print(f"K={k}: Inertia={kmeans.inertia_:.2f}")

# The elbow is typically around K=3-4
hints: Use a loop to test multiple K values
Look for the elbow point where inertia stops decreasing rapidly

title: DBSCAN Clustering
description: Apply DBSCAN clustering to the iris dataset and identify outliers.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Apply DBSCAN with eps=0.5 and min_samples=5
# TODO: Count the number of clusters and outliers
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply DBSCAN
dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=5)
labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# Count clusters and outliers
n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
n_outliers = list(labels).count(-1)

print(f"Number of clusters: {n_clusters}")
print(f"Number of outliers: {n_outliers}")
print(f"Unique labels: {sorted(set(labels))}")
hints: Count unique labels and subtract 1 if -1 is present

title: PCA for Dimensionality Reduction
description: Apply PCA to reduce iris dataset from 4D to 2D and visualize explained variance.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Apply PCA with 2 components
# TODO: Print explained variance ratio
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply PCA with 2 components
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

print(f"Explained variance ratio: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_}")
print(f"Total variance explained: {sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_):.2%}")
print(f"PCA components shape: {X_pca.shape}")
print(f"First component explains: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.2%}")
hints: Sum of ratios tells total variance retained

title: Silhouette Score for Cluster Evaluation
description: Calculate silhouette score for K-Means clustering with different K values.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Test K from 2 to 8 and calculate silhouette score
# TODO: Find which K has the best silhouette score
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

silhouette_scores = []
K_range = range(2, 9)

for k in K_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
    score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)
    silhouette_scores.append(score)
    print(f"K={k}: Silhouette={score:.3f}")

best_k = K_range[silhouette_scores.index(max(silhouette_scores))]
print(f"\nBest K: {best_k}")

title: Hierarchical Clustering
description: Apply agglomerative hierarchical clustering to iris dataset.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Apply AgglomerativeClustering with 3 clusters
# TODO: Print cluster labels and unique clusters
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply hierarchical clustering
hierarchical = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=3, linkage='ward')
labels = hierarchical.fit_predict(X_scaled)

print(f"Number of clusters: {len(set(labels))}")
print(f"Cluster distribution: {np.bincount(labels)}")
print(f"First 10 labels: {labels[:10]}")

title: PCA Explained Variance Analysis
description: Find how many PCA components needed to explain 95% of variance.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Use PCA with all 4 components
# TODO: Find cumulative variance explained
# TODO: Determine how many components needed for 95% variance
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# PCA with all components
pca = PCA()
pca.fit(X_scaled)

# Calculate cumulative variance
cumsum = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
print("Cumulative variance ratio:")
for i, val in enumerate(cumsum):
    print(f"Component {i+1}: {val:.2%}")

# Find components needed for 95%
n_components_95 = np.argmax(cumsum >= 0.95) + 1
print(f"\nComponents needed for 95% variance: {n_components_95}")
hints: Use np.argmax to find threshold crossing
95% is common threshold for dimensionality reduction

title: Feature Scaling Impact on K-Means
description: Compare K-Means results with and without scaling on iris dataset.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data

# TODO: K-Means WITHOUT scaling
# TODO: K-Means WITH scaling
# TODO: Compare inertias and see how scaling affects clustering
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data

# K-Means without scaling
kmeans_unscaled = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
kmeans_unscaled.fit(X)
inertia_unscaled = kmeans_unscaled.inertia_

# K-Means with scaling
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
kmeans_scaled = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
kmeans_scaled.fit(X_scaled)
inertia_scaled = kmeans_scaled.inertia_

print(f"Inertia (unscaled): {inertia_unscaled:.2f}")
print(f"Inertia (scaled): {inertia_scaled:.2f}")
print(f"\nUnscaled labels match rate: {sum(kmeans_unscaled.labels_ == kmeans_unscaled.labels_) / len(X):.2%}")
print("Note: Scaling changes results because larger-scale features dominate distance.")
hints: Compare inertia values before and after scaling

title: Isolation Forest for Anomaly Detection
description: Use Isolation Forest to detect anomalies in iris dataset.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Apply IsolationForest with contamination=0.1
# TODO: Count and identify anomalies
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply Isolation Forest
iso_forest = IsolationForest(contamination=0.1, random_state=42)
predictions = iso_forest.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# Count anomalies (labeled as -1)
n_anomalies = sum(predictions == -1)
n_normal = sum(predictions == 1)

print(f"Normal points: {n_normal}")
print(f"Anomalies: {n_anomalies}")
print(f"Anomaly indices: {np.where(predictions == -1)[0]}")
print(f"\nAnomaly scores (lower = more anomalous):")
scores = iso_forest.score_samples(X_scaled)
for idx in np.where(predictions == -1)[0]:
    print(f"  Sample {idx}: {scores[idx]:.3f}")
hints: IsolationForest returns -1 for anomalies, 1 for normal
contamination parameter is ratio of anomalies

title: Comparing Clustering Algorithms
description: Apply K-Means, DBSCAN, and Hierarchical clustering, compare silhouette scores.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, DBSCAN, AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# TODO: Apply all three clustering algorithms
# TODO: Calculate silhouette score for each
# TODO: Compare results
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans, DBSCAN, AgglomerativeClustering
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# K-Means
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
labels_kmeans = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
score_kmeans = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels_kmeans)

# DBSCAN
dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=5)
labels_dbscan = dbscan.fit_predict(X_scaled)
if len(set(labels_dbscan)) > 1:
    score_dbscan = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels_dbscan)
else:
    score_dbscan = -1

# Hierarchical
hierarchical = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=3, linkage='ward')
labels_hier = hierarchical.fit_predict(X_scaled)
score_hier = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels_hier)

print(f"K-Means Silhouette:      {score_kmeans:.3f}")
print(f"DBSCAN Silhouette:       {score_dbscan:.3f}")
print(f"Hierarchical Silhouette: {score_hier:.3f}")
print(f"\nBest algorithm: {max(('K-Means', score_kmeans), ('DBSCAN', score_dbscan), ('Hierarchical', score_hier), key=lambda x: x[1])[0]}")
hints: DBSCAN may produce single cluster or outliers
